description: Add comprehensive unit tests for side panel modules to catch data-flow bugs like the browserTools.length TypeError before they ship. Follows the established pattern: test pure/extracted logic in Node.js without jsdom or React rendering. Covers useServerNotifications hook, bridge defensive guards, App.tsx loadPlugins logic, BrowserToolsCard toggle logic, PluginCard toggle logic, SearchResults filtering, and NpmPluginCard helpers.

userStories.title: Unit tests for useServerNotifications hook logic
userStories.description: As a developer, I want comprehensive unit tests for the useServerNotifications hook so that notification handling, timeout management, and input validation are verified.
acceptanceCriteria: Tests confirmation.request: valid confirmation creates entry via setPendingConfirmations, auto-removal fires after timeoutMs+1000, clearConfirmationTimeout cancels pending timeout, missing id/tool/timeoutMs fields are ignored

Tests tab.stateChanged: valid state change calls setPlugins updater, invalid tab states are ignored, invalid plugin names (fails VALID_PLUGIN_NAME regex) are ignored, tab state buffered in pendingTabStates when plugins array is empty
Tests tool.invocationStart: adds tool key to activeTools, auto-removal after TOOL_INVOCATION_TIMEOUT_MS, replaces existing timeout for same tool key
Tests tool.invocationEnd: removes tool key from activeTools, clears existing invocation timeout
Tests cleanup: unmount clears all confirmation and invocation timeouts
Tests that unknown notification methods are silently ignored
userStories.notes:
The hook is in platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/hooks/useServerNotifications.ts. It returns { handleNotification, clearConfirmationTimeout }.

Testing approach: Since the project uses pure-function testing (no jsdom, no React rendering), test the hook by calling it through a lightweight harness. Create mock setter functions (vi.fn()) for setPlugins, setActiveTools, setPendingConfirmations, and a mock pendingTabStates ref. Then invoke the hook's returned handleNotification directly with test payloads.

The hook uses useCallback, useEffect, and useRef internally. To test without React rendering, extract the core notification-handling logic into a separate pure function (e.g., processNotification) that takes the setters and refs as arguments and returns the handler. The hook then becomes a thin wrapper. This matches the established pattern in the codebase (ConfirmationDialog exports resolveDisplayIndex, PluginIcon exports hashString/getAvatarVar/getAvatarLetter).

Alternatively, since the hook's handleNotification callback is essentially a pure function over its closure variables (the setters), you can test it by:
1. Creating a test helper that mimics the hook's closure: creates the timeout maps, wraps handleNotification in a function that takes the same params
2. Using vi.useFakeTimers() for timeout tests
3. Asserting on the mock setters' calls

Key test data:
- VALID_PLUGIN_NAME regex is imported from '../../constants.js' — use valid names like 'slack', 'my-plugin' and invalid ones like '' or names with spaces
- Valid tab states: 'closed', 'unavailable', 'ready'
- TOOL_INVOCATION_TIMEOUT_MS is 300000 (5 min)

Mock chrome.runtime.sendMessage for the confirmationTimeout case (line 85: chrome.runtime.sendMessage({ type: 'sp:confirmationTimeout', id }).catch(() => {})).

Important: The test file must be .test.ts (not .test.tsx). All imports use .js extensions. Import vi, describe, test, expect, beforeEach, afterEach from 'vitest'.

userStories.title: Unit tests for loadPlugins defensive data handling
userStories.description: As a developer, I want unit tests that verify the side panel handles malformed or partial config.getState responses without crashing, so that TypeError bugs like the browserTools.length crash are caught at the test level.
acceptanceCriteria: Tests that fetchConfigState resolves with the server response result field (existing coverage verified)
New tests for a configStateNormalize helper function that guards undefined plugins, failedPlugins, browserTools fields with ?? []
Tests that plugins with missing tools field get normalized to tools: []
Tests that a completely empty result ({}) normalizes to { plugins: [], failedPlugins: [], browserTools: [] }
Tests that a valid result passes through unchanged
Tests that null values for array fields are normalized to []

userStories.title: Unit tests for SearchResults filtering logic
userStories.description: As a developer, I want unit tests for the SearchResults filtering and deduplication logic so that search behavior is verified independently of rendering.
acceptanceCriteria: Tests matchesBrowserTool: matches on display name (converted from snake_case), raw name, and description; case-insensitive; partial substring match; does not match unrelated queries
Tests installed vs available deduplication: npm results whose extractShortName matches an installed plugin's extractShortName are filtered out
Tests empty filter shows all installed plugins
Tests showNoResults logic: true only when toolFilter is set AND no installed matches AND not searching AND no available results
userStories.notes:
SearchResults.tsx is at platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/SearchResults.tsx.

The matchesBrowserTool function (line 33) is currently defined inside the file but not exported. Export it so it can be tested. Also extract and export the deduplication logic.

There are several pure-logic paths to test:
1. matchesBrowserTool(tool, filterLower) — tests the browser tool matching logic. Import toDisplayName from BrowserToolsCard.tsx (already exported).
2. Installed-vs-available deduplication — uses extractShortName (already exported from bridge.ts). Test the filtering logic: `npmResults.filter(r => !installedShortNames.has(extractShortName(r.name)))`.
3. showNoResults derivation — `toolFilter && !hasInstalledResults && !npmSearching && availableResults.length === 0`.

To make these testable, export matchesBrowserTool from SearchResults.tsx. The deduplication and showNoResults logic can be tested via factory helpers without React rendering.

Create test data factories following the pattern in bridge.test.ts:
```typescript
const browserTool = (overrides: Partial<BrowserToolState> = {}): BrowserToolState => ({
  name: 'browser_list_tabs',
  description: 'List all open tabs',
  enabled: true,
  ...overrides,
});
```

Test cases:
- matchesBrowserTool: display name match ('List Tabs' matches 'list'), raw name match ('browser_list'), description match, case insensitive, no match
- Deduplication: plugin '@opentabs-dev/opentabs-plugin-slack' (shortName 'slack') filters out npm result 'opentabs-plugin-slack'
- showNoResults: various combinations of toolFilter, hasInstalledResults, npmSearching, availableResults.length

userStories.title: Unit tests for BrowserToolsCard toggle and display logic
userStories.description: As a developer, I want unit tests for the BrowserToolsCard's toDisplayName conversion and allEnabled derivation so that browser tool display names and toggle-all behavior are verified.
acceptanceCriteria: Tests toDisplayName: 'browser_list_tabs' → 'List Tabs', 'browser_screenshot_tab' → 'Screenshot Tab', 'extension_get_state' → 'Extension Get State' (no prefix removal for non-browser_ names), 'browser_get_page_html' → 'Get Page Html', handles empty string
Tests allEnabled derivation: true only when tools.length > 0 AND every tool is enabled; false when any tool disabled; false when tools array is empty
Tests visible tools filtering: filterLower filters by display name, raw name, and description
userStories.notes:
BrowserToolsCard.tsx is at platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/BrowserToolsCard.tsx. toDisplayName is already exported (line 138).

The allEnabled logic at line 45 is inline: `tools.length > 0 && tools.every(t => t.enabled)`. Extract this into a named exported function for testability:
```typescript
const allToolsEnabled = (tools: BrowserToolState[]): boolean => tools.length > 0 && tools.every(t => t.enabled);
```

The visible tools filter at lines 74-81 is also inline. Extract and export:
```typescript
const filterBrowserTools = (tools: BrowserToolState[], filterLower: string): BrowserToolState[] =>
  filterLower
    ? tools.filter(t =>
        toDisplayName(t.name).toLowerCase().includes(filterLower) ||
        t.name.toLowerCase().includes(filterLower) ||
        t.description.toLowerCase().includes(filterLower)
      )
    : tools;
```

Test cases for toDisplayName:
1. 'browser_list_tabs' → 'List Tabs'
2. 'browser_screenshot_tab' → 'Screenshot Tab'
3. 'browser_get_page_html' → 'Get Page Html'
4. 'extension_get_state' → 'Extension Get State' (no browser_ prefix to strip)
5. 'browser_' → '' (just prefix, no words after)
6. '' → '' (empty string edge case)

Test cases for allToolsEnabled:
1. All enabled → true
2. One disabled → false
3. All disabled → false
4. Empty array → false
5. Single enabled tool → true

Test cases for filterBrowserTools:
1. Empty filter returns all tools
2. Filter by display name ('screenshot' matches 'browser_screenshot_tab')
3. Filter by raw name ('browser_list' matches)
4. Filter by description
5. No match returns empty array
6. Case insensitive

userStories.title: Unit tests for PluginCard allEnabled derivation and tool filtering
userStories.description: As a developer, I want unit tests for the PluginCard's allEnabled derivation and visible-tools filtering so that plugin tool state and search behavior are verified.
acceptanceCriteria: Tests allEnabled derivation: true only when tools.length > 0 AND every tool is enabled; false when any tool disabled; false when tools is empty; handles tools being undefined (via ?? [] guard)
Tests visible tools filtering with matchesTool (already tested in bridge.test.ts, but verify the integration path)
userStories.notes:
PluginCard.tsx is at platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/PluginCard.tsx.

The allEnabled logic at line 52: `plugin.tools.length > 0 && plugin.tools.every(t => t.enabled)`. Extract a testable function:
```typescript
const allPluginToolsEnabled = (tools: PluginState['tools']): boolean => tools.length > 0 && tools.every(t => t.enabled);
```

Export it from PluginCard.tsx and test it in PluginCard.test.ts.

Also extract and export the visible tools filtering logic (line 80):
```typescript
const filterPluginTools = (tools: PluginState['tools'], filterLower: string): PluginState['tools'] =>
  filterLower ? tools.filter(t => matchesTool(t, filterLower)) : tools;
```

Create a WireToolDef factory:
```typescript
const tool = (overrides: Partial<WireToolDef> = {}): WireToolDef => ({
  name: 'send_message',
  displayName: 'Send Message',
  description: 'Send a message',
  icon: 'message-square',
  enabled: true,
  ...overrides,
});
```

Test cases for allPluginToolsEnabled:
1. All tools enabled → true
2. One tool disabled → false
3. All tools disabled → false
4. Empty tools array → false
5. Single enabled tool → true

Test cases for filterPluginTools:
1. Empty filter returns all tools
2. Filter matches displayName
3. Filter matches name
4. Filter matches description
5. No match returns empty

userStories.title: Unit tests for NpmPluginCard deriveDisplayName
userStories.description: As a developer, I want unit tests for the NpmPluginCard deriveDisplayName function so that npm package name display logic is verified.
acceptanceCriteria: Tests deriveDisplayName: scoped package '@opentabs-dev/opentabs-plugin-slack' → 'slack', unscoped 'opentabs-plugin-datadog' → 'datadog', bare name 'custom-tool' → 'custom-tool', scoped without prefix '@scope/my-plugin' → 'my-plugin', empty string → ''
userStories.notes:
NpmPluginCard.tsx is at platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/NpmPluginCard.tsx. The deriveDisplayName function (line 22) is currently NOT exported. Export it:
```typescript
export { NpmPluginCard, deriveDisplayName };
```

Test cases:
1. '@opentabs-dev/opentabs-plugin-slack' → 'slack'
2. 'opentabs-plugin-datadog' → 'datadog'
3. 'custom-tool' → 'custom-tool'
4. '@scope/my-plugin' → 'my-plugin'
5. '' → ''
6. '@scope/opentabs-plugin-jira' → 'jira'

This is very similar to extractShortName in bridge.ts. The difference is that deriveDisplayName does NOT handle the case where the package name has no scope — it just uses split('/').pop() which handles both cases. Verify the behavior matches extractShortName for the common cases, and note any differences.

This is a small story — keep it focused. Follow the pattern in PluginIcon.test.ts (small, focused, pure function tests).

userStories.title: Unit tests for PluginList filtering and visibility logic
userStories.description: As a developer, I want unit tests for the PluginList's filtering logic so that plugin visibility during search is verified.
acceptanceCriteria: Tests visible plugins filtering: filterLower filters plugins by matchesPlugin (tools.some via matchesTool); empty filter returns all plugins
Tests failed plugins visibility: failed plugins hidden when filterLower is set, shown when filter is empty
Tests empty results: returns 'no tools matching' condition when filterLower is set and no plugins match
Tests that plugin with undefined tools does not crash the filter (defense in depth)
userStories.notes:
PluginList.tsx is at platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/PluginList.tsx. The filtering logic at lines 27-32 is inline.

Extract and export these as pure functions:
```typescript
const filterVisiblePlugins = (plugins: PluginState[], filterLower: string): PluginState[] =>
  filterLower ? plugins.filter(p => matchesPlugin(p, filterLower)) : plugins;

const filterVisibleFailed = (failedPlugins: FailedPluginState[], filterLower: string): FailedPluginState[] =>
  filterLower ? [] : failedPlugins;
```

Test cases for filterVisiblePlugins:
1. Empty filter returns all plugins
2. Filter matches plugin name — returns matching plugin
3. Filter matches plugin displayName — returns matching plugin
4. Filter matches tool name within plugin — returns the plugin
5. No plugins match — returns empty array
6. Multiple plugins, some match — returns only matching ones
7. Plugin with empty tools array — only matches on name/displayName

Test cases for filterVisibleFailed:
1. Empty filter returns all failed plugins
2. Non-empty filter returns empty array (failed plugins hidden during search)

Create a plugin factory:
```typescript
const plugin = (overrides: Partial<PluginState> = {}): PluginState => ({
  name: 'slack',
  displayName: 'Slack',
  version: '1.0.0',
  trustTier: 'official' as const,
  source: 'npm' as const,
  tabState: 'closed' as const,
  urlPatterns: ['*://*.slack.com/*'],
  tools: [],
  ...overrides,
});
```

userStories.title: Update tsconfig.test.json include pattern for .test.tsx files
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the test TypeScript config to cover .test.tsx files so that future React component tests are type-checked.
acceptanceCriteria: All existing .test.ts files continue to pass type-check
userStories.notes:
platform/browser-extension/tsconfig.test.json currently has:
```json
"include": ["src/**/*.test.ts"]
```

Update to:
```json
"include": ["src/**/*.test.ts", "src/**/*.test.tsx"]
```

Also check platform/browser-extension/tsconfig.json exclude pattern — it currently has:
```json
"exclude": ["src/**/*.test.ts", "src/**/*.stories.tsx"]
```

Update to:
```json
"exclude": ["src/**/*.test.ts", "src/**/*.test.tsx", "src/**/*.stories.tsx"]
```

Also check the root vitest.config.ts — it currently includes 'platform/**/*.test.ts'. If any new test files use .test.tsx extension, update the vitest include to: ['platform/**/*.test.{ts,tsx}']. However, since all the tests in this PRD are .test.ts (pure function tests, no JSX needed), this vitest change may not be needed yet. Only update vitest config if a .test.tsx file is actually created.

This is a small infrastructure story to unblock future React component tests. Keep it minimal.
